data.title: BP considering sale of U.K. North Sea assets - Bloomberg
data.description:
BP (BP) is considering a potential exit of all or part of its operations in the U.K. North Sea, Bloomberg reported Friday, as it works to dispose of assets and pay down debt.

The company is conducting an internal review of its upstream operations in the U.K., which could fetch ~£2B ($2.7B) in a full divestment, according to the report.

BP (BP) is among the last remaining oil majors operating in the U.K. North Sea, as peers such as Chevron and ConocoPhillips sold assets, while others including Shell, Exxon, and TotalEnergies have moved to divest or restructure parts of their positions.

BP (BP) still holds a 45% stake in the Clair Field—the largest oil field on the U.K. Continental Shelf—but the company has been reducing its presence in the region for years, including sales of its interest in the Shearwater field to Shell and the Forties pipeline system to Ineos.

data.title: Matthews targets at least $180m adjusted EBITDA in fiscal 2026 while planning a Propelis exit in 12-18 months
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Matthews International Corporation (MATW) Q2 fiscal 2026

Management view

  • "On our last earnings call, we said that we were focused on execution, and we did just that in the second quarter," said "The redemption of our high-cost notes is complete. Our balance sheet is significantly improved. Interest expense is down materially." (CEO, President & Director Joseph Bartolacci)

  • "In January, we completed the early redemption of our $300 million of senior secured notes," and "Our total long-term debt is now $579 million, down from $822 million 1 year ago," while "Net debt stands at approximately $543 million today." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • "Propelis is continuing to perform well above the $100 million EBITDA run rate that was assumed when we structured the transaction," and "their EBITDA run rate is expected to be around $130 million going into 2027," with "an exit from this investment within the next 12 to 18 months." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • "We shipped our first production units to paying customers," and "the commercial response to Acxiom remains strong," while management "expanded our total addressable market estimate to about $3 billion" and said it "continue[s] to actively pursue and engage in strategic partnership discussions, including white label opportunities." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • "For the fiscal 2026 second quarter, the company reported a net loss of $21.8 million or $0.69 per share," and "On a non-GAAP adjusted basis, net income attributable to the company for the current quarter was $11.6 million or $0.37 per share." (CFO & Treasurer Daniel Stopar)

Outlook

  • "With regard to our full year outlook, we set guidance of at least $180 million in adjusted EBITDA for fiscal 2026, inclusive of our 40% interest in Propelis," and "we are reaffirming our full year adjusted EBITDA guidance of $180 million." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • "Achieving the full year target requires a stronger second half," with drivers cited as "Memorialization continuing its current trajectory, Industrial Technologies converting its pipeline and Propelis continuous operational execution." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • "Several things may impact that forecast," including "The pace and timing of engineering orders, the outcome of current tariff discussions at the federal level, the timing of synergies at Propelis us and the economic impact of geopolitical challenges." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • Analysts’ EPS and revenue estimates were provided, but the JSON structure did not include a valid quarter format (Q1-Q4), so no estimates comparison is included.

Financial results

  • "Total revenues were $259 million compared to $428 million a year ago," and "Adjusted EBITDA for the fiscal 2026 second quarter was $45 million compared to $51 million in the prior year second quarter." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • "Consolidated adjusted EBITDA for the fiscal 2026 second quarter was $44.7 million compared to $51.4 million a year ago," and "The decline reflected lower operating performance by the Engineering business within the Industrial Technologies segment." (CFO & Treasurer Stopar)

  • "Our Cornerstone segment reported sales of $215 million for the second quarter," with "adjusted EBITDA of $49 million," and "The Dodge acquisition continues to contribute meaningfully, adding approximately $10 million in sales per quarter and is ahead of our EBITDA targets." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • "Cash flow used in operating activities for the 6 months ended March 31, 2026, was $67.4 million," and management cited cash uses including "income taxes, transaction fees and repayments of securitized receivables" and that "Expenditures for litigation and proxy defense also consumed significant cash." (CFO & Treasurer Stopar)

Q&A

  • Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.: Asked about organic Memorialization growth beyond Dodge and whether Matthews is seeing more M&A inbound; CEO, President & Director Bartolacci replied, "our volume [is] stable to modestly down" and said M&A interest exists but "I wouldn't say there's a lot of inbounds, but there are opportunities out there. We'll pick timing based on when it's right for us."

  • Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.: Asked for more detail on Propelis SAP execution timing; CEO, President & Director Bartolacci said, "we are at the middle" and "they will start that migration in about 90 days," adding, "we're very confident on their ability to execute going forward."

  • Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.: Asked about next steps after the Tesla arbitration decision and whether customer engagement improved; CEO, President & Director Bartolacci said, "it's given a lot of clarity" and "they have opened more doors in the last 60 days or so," adding, "We have expanded our geographies to include Japan" and "we are emboldened by it."

  • Colin Rusch, Oppenheimer: Asked about how quickly ultracapacitor opportunity could come and breadth of customer participation; CEO, President & Director Bartolacci said, "We have the 3 largest producers of ultracapacitors at our doorstep today" and described "multiple forms of partnership" plus a Germany-based production system "being commissioned as we speak."

  • Colin Rusch, Oppenheimer: Asked about reshoring-driven battery manufacturing demand and military-linked supply chain requirements; CEO, President & Director Bartolacci said, "We're having significant discussions with solid-state manufacturers" and referenced "a relatively large order for North America battery separators" expected "over the course of the next 3 months, 4 months or so."

  • Justin Bergner, Gabelli Funds, LLC: Asked to confirm Dodge revenue contribution and divestiture impacts; CFO & Treasurer Stopar replied, "$166 million from the divestitures" and confirmed the "$11 million contribution" from Dodge referenced in prepared remarks.

  • Justin Bergner, Gabelli Funds, LLC: Asked why Propelis equity-method figures appeared below a $100 million-plus run rate; CFO & Treasurer Stopar said, "Their slowest quarter is typically the fourth calendar quarter."

  • Justin Bergner, Gabelli Funds, LLC: Asked whether Memorialization beat expectations and about inflation/average-cost inventory timing; CEO, President & Director Bartolacci said, "the quarter actually performed better at an execution level, worse at a revenue level," adding, "Price is consistent with what we had expected and -- but execution was even better."

  • Justin Bergner, Gabelli Funds, LLC: Asked about tariffs embedded in forecasts and whether headwinds increased; CEO, President & Director Bartolacci replied, "Modest, yes," and said, "There's an expectation in our forecast for some impact of that."

  • Justin Bergner, Gabelli Funds, LLC: Asked for key one-time cash cost buckets and strategic review costs; CFO & Treasurer Stopar cited "tax payments," "deal fees" and settling "securitized receivables," and later said, "Yes, we're about $55 million" on securitized receivables; CEO, President & Director Bartolacci added, "there's no ongoing costs associated with that" strategic review.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, pressing on cash usage, tariff exposure, Propelis timing/seasonality, and the practical impact of the Tesla ruling (e.g., "what are the next steps" and "are there examples... regarding engagement with new potential customers").

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more assertive in Q&A on legal/IP and execution, including "this ruling removes a key overhang" and "we are emboldened by it," while maintaining caution on near-term contributors ("Action will not be a material contributor to the top line this year"). (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • Versus the prior quarter, management language shifted from announcing leverage progress and divestiture execution to emphasizing post-redemption flexibility and legal clarity around DBE, while keeping the same full-year adjusted EBITDA guidance level. (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • In Q2, management emphasized completion and financial impact of the note redemption ("interest expense is down materially" and "reducing annual interest expense by approximately $10 million"), while Q1 framed the redemption as expected to "reduce our annual interest expense by $12 million." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • Product Identification messaging progressed from Q1’s paused shipments for refinements to Q2 commercialization, with management reporting, "we shipped our first production units to paying customers" after resolving beta issues. (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • The Propelis timeline tightened in language from Q1’s "18- to 24-month window" to Q2’s "12 to 18 months" for an expected exit, alongside a new point that Matthews "expect[s] to receive a partial redemption of our preferred interest in the coming quarter." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • Analysts’ Q2 questions concentrated on Propelis SAP execution, tariff assumptions, and Tesla-arbitration implications, while Q1 questions leaned more toward balance sheet optimization and energy pipeline cadence. (Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.; Colin Rusch, Oppenheimer; Justin Bergner, Gabelli Funds, LLC)

Risks and concerns

  • Management cited variables that could affect the full-year outlook: "The pace and timing of engineering orders," "the outcome of current tariff discussions," "the timing of synergies at Propelis," and "the economic impact of geopolitical challenges." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • Industrial Technologies remained flagged as the swing factor, with management noting the segment "remains challenged" and planning "additional cost reduction actions within the engineering business in the second half to protect cash." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • Cash flow pressure in the first half was attributed to discrete items and seasonality, with management stating, "We expect both Q3 and Q4 to generate positive operating cash flow." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

Final takeaway

Management framed Q2 as execution on balance-sheet repair and a return to clearer second-half planning, anchored by reaffirmed fiscal 2026 adjusted EBITDA guidance of $180 million. Leadership highlighted Memorialization’s continued EBITDA growth and Dodge integration progress, while positioning Propelis as a near-term cash catalyst (preferred redemption) and a targeted exit within 12 to 18 months. Industrial Technologies was characterized as the key variable, with first commercial shipments in Product Identification, a growing engineering pipeline, continued partnership discussions, and a favorable interim arbitration outcome on DBE that management said has reduced customer hesitation and expanded engagement.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Paramount Skydance, Warner Bros. merger a 'big, bold, and game-changing move'—Morgan Stanley
data.description:
Morgan Stanley delivered a double upgrade to Paramount Skydance (PSKY) as the proposed acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) presents a “big, bold, and game-changing move” that positions the company with some of the best intellectual properties in the world, including Game of Thrones, Harry Potter, and Lord of the Rings.

The firm now rates the entertainment conglomerate as Overweight from a prior Underweight rating and lifts the target price to $14, representing 37% upside to Thursday’s close.

While analyst Sean Diffley considers the double upgrade the firm’s “riskiest and most out-of-consensus call,” he considers three underappreciated elements to the PSKY/WBD merger, including the potential for AI to unlock significant cost savings and increase ad revenue, confidence in achieving $6B of synergies, and a clear mix shift to faster-growing streaming and studio assets.

Diffley acknowledges that as there are regulatory hurdles to the deal being consummated, his upgrade is contingent on approval. However, arguments in favor of regulatory approval include the lack of dominant market share in any vertical and a compelling case that the combined company would be pro-consumer, pro-creative talent, and pro-competition.

If, however, the deal doesn’t go through, a standalone Paramount Skydance (PSKY) is much less compelling than a combined company.

The upgrade breathed life into Paramount Skydance (PSKY) shares, snapping a six-day losing streak with a gain of more than 8%.

data.title: Revolution Medicines wins FDA nod to provide early access to cancer therapy
data.description:
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has permitted Revolution Medicines (RVMD) to make its promising investigational therapy, daraxonrasib, available to patients with pancreatic cancer outside a clinical trial setting, the company announced on Friday.

The oral therapy designed to target cancer-causing mutations in the “RAS” family of genes represents a new class of cancer therapies developed by companies including Revolution Medicines (RVMD) and Erasca (ERAS).

Daraxonrasib has already generated promising late-stage trial data indicating its potential as a late-line option for metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma.

The so-called expanded access treatment protocol allows Revolution Medicines (RVMD) to provide daraxonrasib to patients with metastatic PDAC in a controlled and monitored setting.

Announcing promising late-stage trial data for the RAS((ON)) inhibitor earlier this month, the California-based biotech said that it will submit a marketing application seeking an expedited review of daraxonrasib under the Commissioner's National Priority Voucher.

data.title: Intel looks to benefit from rising demand in standard compute: Wedbush
data.description:
Quant Cloud Technology is reportedly seeing a surge in standard compute demand, which is being driven by Google (GOOG)(GOOGL), according to DigiTimes Asia.

If so, Wedbush analysts indicated this would prove more beneficial for Intel (INTC) rather than AMD (AMD).

"Even during our trip to Taiwan in early April, while general CPU/server demand was highlighted as seeing a continuation of the sustained growth realized in 2H'25, component vendors and ODMs we met with didn't call out a sudden expansion in demand," said Wedbush analysts Matt Bryson and Antoine Legault in an investor note.

"However, Quanta is apparently now talking to a surge in standard compute demand," they noted. "A lift in Q2 would also fit with our recent conversations with the supply chain, which suggest that secondary market pricing for CPUs has spiked in recent weeks, signaling a more severe constraint (vs. our previous conversation, which kept coming back to DRAM as the limiting factor for supply)."

The Taiwan-based Quanta is a global provider of data center hardware and infrastructure software.

"We see Intel as the primary beneficiary of this setup, given its ability to add capacity vs. AMD, which we believe should also benefit, but necessarily has less control over its output given its reliance on TSMC (TSM) wafers," Bryson added.

data.title: Acco Brands reiterates 2026 adjusted EPS of $0.84 to $0.89 as EPOS integration targets about $80M sales
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ACCO Brands (ACCO) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "Last night, we reported first quarter results with sales and adjusted EPS above our outlook. We also reiterated full-year guidance," said (President, CEO & Director Thomas Tedford), adding that "first quarter consolidated sales grew 8%" and the company is "executing well on our key operational and strategic initiatives."

  • (President, CEO & Director Thomas Tedford) highlighted category and seasonal drivers, saying, "Sales for computer accessories within the segment were strong, reflecting new products and a meaningful end-user pipeline," and "in North America, early purchases of back-to-school products were better than anticipated." He added, "For the season, we are expecting back-to-school sales to be flat to up low single digits."

  • On portfolio strategy and EPOS, (President, CEO & Director Thomas Tedford) said, "Our overall strategy remains focused on expanding our product range in faster-growing categories with an emphasis on technology peripherals," and specified, "Our target for 2026 is for peripherals to grow to represent 25% of the company's projected revenue." He also announced, "As a result of the acquisition, Jeppe Dalberg-Larsen, President of EPOS, will now lead Technology peripherals for ACCO Brands."

  • (Executive VP & CFO Deborah OConnor) detailed acquisition accounting and integration milestones: "This $38 million gain represents the purchase price of EPOS compared to the preliminary fair market value of the business, which is primarily from working capital," and "our outlook includes $80 million of 2026 sales with a slightly higher gross profit rate than our consolidated average and neutral to adjusted EPS." She added, "We remain on track to deliver the outlined $15 million in cost synergies in 12 to 18 months," and "We recorded $7 million in restructuring charges, primarily related to this acquisition, most of which will be paid out in the next year."

Outlook

  • (Executive VP & CFO Deborah OConnor) reaffirmed full-year guidance: "For 2026, we are reiterating our expectation for full year reported sales to be flat to up 3% and adjusted EPS to be within the range of $0.84 to $0.89," adding, "This outlook reflects a prudent sales expectation in the back half of the year given the global environment."

  • (Executive VP & CFO Deborah OConnor) gave cash flow, CapEx, restructuring, and leverage expectations: "Free cash flow is expected to be within the range of $75 million to $85 million, with approximately $25 million in restructuring payments and $15 million in CapEx," and "Lastly, we anticipate a consolidated leverage ratio within a range of 3.7x to 3.9x."

  • For Q2, (Executive VP & CFO Deborah OConnor) guided, "we expect reported sales to be up within a range of 1% to 4%" and "adjusted earnings per share to be within the range of $0.24 to $0.28," while noting "a lesser benefit from FX."

  • Versus last quarter’s messaging, management maintained the same full-year ranges previously issued, while adding a more explicit cost-risk framing tied to geopolitical conditions; (President, CEO & Director Thomas Tedford) said Q1 savings "may be offset by rising costs due to the ongoing conflict in the Middle East" and the company "anticipate fuel costs and certain raw materials to increase globally with the impact weighted towards the back half of the year."

Financial results

  • (Executive VP & CFO Deborah OConnor) reported, "Reported sales in the first quarter increased 8% with comparable sales down less than 3%," and said growth was "driven by FX and the EPOS acquisition."

  • (Executive VP & CFO Deborah OConnor) stated profitability metrics: "Gross profit for the first quarter was $107 million, an increase of 7%, with the margin rate of 31.1%, down 30 basis points," and "Adjusted operating income for the first quarter was $12 million, up $5 million versus the prior year."

  • (Executive VP & CFO Deborah OConnor) described segment performance: "In the Americas segment, sales were up 3% with comparable sales down 2%," while "In the International segment for the first quarter, sales were up 15%, with comparable sales down approximately 3%."

  • (Executive VP & CFO Deborah OConnor) reported balance sheet and cash flow items: "Free cash flow in the quarter was $1.4 million," "Inventory was up $67 million since the start of the year" (including "$27 million" related to EPOS), and "finished the quarter with a consolidated leverage ratio of 4.1x" with "approximately $252 million available for borrowing under our revolver."

Q&A

  • Gregory Burns, Sidoti & Company: asked why there was not more flow-through to full-year guidance after Q1 outperformance and requested the FX/acquisition contribution; (Executive VP & CFO OConnor) replied, "the first quarter for us is a pretty small quarter" and said EPOS is "about 5%" of full-year sales with FX "about 1%."

  • Gregory Burns, Sidoti & Company: asked about EPOS growth opportunities and why it had not expanded previously; (President, CEO & Director Tedford) answered, "We are early in the integration process with EPOS," added "we think there's opportunities... to offer a one-stop solution," and said, "I don't want to comment on the historical performance of EPOS."

  • Joseph Gomes, NOBLE Capital Markets: asked what drove EPOS outperformance vs expectations; (President, CEO & Director Tedford) said, "we weren't really sure the uncertainty of an acquired business" and "we just found it prudent to be careful with our guidance assumptions for the business."

  • Joseph Gomes, NOBLE Capital Markets: asked for color on early back-to-school; (President, CEO & Director Tedford) said, "we believe the season is going to be up modestly" and added, "we have good line of sight to the initial orders. They're at or better than our current forecast."

  • Kevin Steinke, Barrington Research: asked about Latin America go-to-market changes and gaming headwinds; (President, CEO & Director Tedford) said, "We've adjusted our product assortment... incentive plans... and we've adjusted pricing," and on gaming said holiday was "relatively weak" creating retailer inventory pressure, while "our brand has taken share each month in the first 3 months of the quarter."

  • Kevin Steinke, Barrington Research: asked whether demand softening is already visible and about pricing; (President, CEO & Director Tedford) responded, "We haven't to date" and "We do have some planned price increases... and we'll continue to monitor the cost environment."

  • William Reuter, BofA Securities: asked about potential tariff refunds and oil/fuel cost headwinds; (Executive VP & CFO OConnor) said the claim is "somewhere in kind of the $25 million range" and "We don't expect anything in 2026," while (President, CEO & Director Tedford) added, "We've built our best thinking into our current guidance" given a "dynamic situation" tied to the Middle East conflict.

  • William Reuter, BofA Securities: asked what is included in the 25% peripherals goal and competitive dynamics; (President, CEO & Director Tedford) said, "It consists of our brands, Kensington, PowerA, LucidSound and EPOS" and added, "We think those are large TAMs, growing TAMs and TAMs in which we have relatively small shares in."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral, pressing on conservatism in guidance and external sensitivities; Burns asked, "given the strong first quarter, why wasn't there more flow-through," and Reuter challenged timing by noting "there are a lot of signs that indicate some refunds may be paid this year."

  • Management sentiment was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more cautious in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing prudence and uncertainty; (Executive VP & CFO OConnor) said, "with all the global uncertainty... we just prudently left... our guidance," and (President, CEO & Director Tedford) referenced "a dynamic situation" and said, "We've built our best thinking into our current guidance."

  • Compared with the prior quarter, management continued to project confidence in the strategy pivot but added more real-time cost inflation language; Q4 emphasized "stabilizing end markets," while Q1 highlighted that savings "may be offset" and that cost increases are "weighted towards the back half of the year."

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance ranges were maintained quarter over quarter; Q1 reiterated "flat to up 3%" sales and adjusted EPS "$0.84 to $0.89," consistent with the prior quarter’s outlook.

  • Leadership participation shifted back to the CFO; Q4 noted "Deb O'Connor... will not be joined today," while Q1 included detailed CFO commentary on EPOS accounting, free cash flow, inventory, and leverage.

  • Strategy framing evolved from peripherals already being about a quarter of projected revenue in Q4 to a stated 2026 target; Q1 said, "Our target for 2026 is for peripherals to grow to represent 25% of the company's projected revenue."

  • Analyst focus remained concentrated on EPOS integration and guidance assumptions, with Q1 adding deeper probing on refunds and energy-driven costs; management more frequently emphasized prudence tied to geopolitical uncertainty than in Q4.

Risks and concerns

  • (President, CEO & Director Tedford) warned that cost savings "may be offset by rising costs due to the ongoing conflict in the Middle East" and said fuel and certain raw material increases are expected "with the impact weighted towards the back half of the year," adding, "The company is carefully monitoring the situation and has taken appropriate steps to mitigate these potential impacts."

  • (Executive VP & CFO Deborah OConnor) cited working-capital and tariff-related pressures in inventory, saying inventory was up due to EPOS, "seasonal inventory build and higher tariff costs."

  • Gaming demand risk was framed as industry and consumer-driven; (President, CEO & Director Tedford) said the market faced "broad industry challenges and softer consumer spending" and that holiday weakness created "inventory opportunities for retailers" that affected Q1.

Final takeaway

ACCO Brands’ management emphasized a Q1 start that came in above its internal outlook while holding 2026 guidance steady, citing a smaller Q1 profit mix and uncertainty around back-half demand and cost inflation. The company positioned EPOS as a key portfolio move toward technology peripherals, expecting about $80 million of 2026 sales over 11 months, $15 million of cost synergies in 12 to 18 months, and a modest profit contribution, while flagging fuel/raw-material and tariff-related variables as swing factors for the remainder of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Allied Gold falls again as Mali conflict futher escalates
data.description:
Allied Gold (AAUC), which is being acquired by China's Zijin Mining (ZIJMF) (ZIJMY), dropped again, falling 1.5%, as a conflict in Mali appeared to intensify. 

Al-Qaeda-led militants are closing in on Mali's capital and forcing a retreat of the Russian mercenaries who were trying to stop the spread of the Islamist group across the country, according to a  WSJ report on Friday. In the capital Bamako, militants imposed a siege, attacked the airport and killed the defense minister. 

Allied Gold (AAUC) shares dropped 4.5% on Monday after the defense minister was killed in a suicide attack by an al-Qaeda affiliate on Saturday.

In the wake of the latest fighting, Allied Gold (AAUC) on Tuesday confirmed the normal course conduct of business  at its operating mines in Mali. 

"In light of recent events in Mali involving the conflicts between government and insurgent groups, the Company also wishes to report that while its business operations in Mali continue in the normal course, with such activities occurring at far distances from the Company’s operations, the Company continues to monitor the situation and take precautions to ensure the safety and wellbeing of persons employed by the Company in the country, Allied Gold (AAUC) said in a statement on Tuesday.

Allied Gold didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's email request for comment on Friday.Zijin Mining (ZIJMF) (ZIJMY) agreed to buy Allied Gold (AAUC) for ~C$5.5B (US$4B) in cash in January.

data.title: Intel granted early termination from FTC for SambaNova Systems partnership
data.description:
• Intel (INTC) was granted an early termination notice on its pending SambaNova Systems partnership, the Federal Trade Commission said on Friday.
  • Santa Clara, Calif.-based Intel said in February that it was taking part in a $350M funding round for the AI chip startup and would also form a partnership with the company.
  • SambaNova Systems raised $350M in Series E financing to expand manufacturing and cloud capacity, with new investors including Vista Equity Partners, Cambium Capital, Intel Capital, Battery Ventures, and accounts advised by T. Rowe Price Associates.
  • The AI hardware and software company said SoftBank (SFTBY) will be the first customer to deploy SN50 within its next‑generation AI data centers in Japan. The SN50 delivers five times more compute per accelerator and four times more network bandwidth than the previous generation, the company added. Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan also serves as SambaNova's executive chairman. 
  • Intel had previously discussed buying the startup for about $1.6B, including debt.

data.title: Google, Nvidia among 8 inking deals with Pentagon for AI use on classified work
data.description:
The U.S. Department of War has entered into agreements with eight technology companies to use their AI capabilities in classified settings, boosting the Pentagon's efforts to get access to cutting-edge AI.

The Department of War, also known as the Department of Defense, inked agreements with ChatGPT maker OpenAI (OPENAI); Alphabet’s (GOOG) (GOOGL) Google; Elon Musk’s SpaceX (SPACE), which owns xAI (X.AI), the maker of AI chatbot Grok; Microsoft (MSFT); Amazon's (AMZN) Amazon Web Services; Nvidia (NVDA); and startup Reflection AI to deploy their advanced AI capabilities on the department's classified networks for lawful operational use.

Following this announcement, the Undersecretary of War for Research and Engineering and Chief Technology Officer Emil Michael noted that Oracle (ORCL) has joined the efforts.

"Following this morning’s announcement, the @DeptofWar is announcing that Oracle has officially agreed to join the list of AI companies deploying frontier capabilities on the Department’s classified networks," said Michael on X.

Shares of Oracle (ORCL) jumped about 7% on Friday.

The DoD said that SpaceX, OpenAI, Google, Nvidia, Reflection, Microsoft, and AWS will provide resources to deploy their capabilities for both Impact Level 6 (IL6) and Impact Level 7 (IL7) environments.

The agency noted that the effort supports its AI Acceleration Strategy by enabling new capabilities across its three core tenets of warfighting, intelligence, and enterprise operations.

GenAI.mil, the department's official AI platform, is already demonstrating the scale and impact of this acceleration. Over 1.3M department personnel have used the platform, according to DoD.

The Department of War declined to comment further but directed Seeking Alpha to a post by Michael on X. "Today, the @DeptofWar entered into agreements with SEVEN of the world's leading frontier AI model and infrastructure companies to deploy frontier capabilities on the Department's classified networks."

"This is just the latest initiative in our mandate to create an AI-FIRST WAR DEPARTMENT," Michael added.

"For more than a decade, AWS has been committed to supporting our nation's military and ensuring that our warfighters and defense partners have access to the best technology at the best value. We look forward to continuing to support the Department of War's modernization efforts, building AI solutions that help them accomplish their critical missions," said AWS spokesperson Tim Barrett in an email to Seeking Alpha.

Google declined to comment beyond the Department's press release in an email to Seeking Alpha. Nvidia declined to comment as well.

Oracle, SpaceX, OpenAI, Reflection, and Microsoft did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

Michael, speaking to CNBC, said that the Department will look at how to work with all the models in the coming year. He added that they cannot rely on only one partner for AI.

The deals show how much of Silicon Valley is agreeing to the DoD's terms in a way that Anthropic (ANTHRO) did not. The Claude AI chatbot maker — which is backed by Amazon (AMZN) and Google — has been in a legal tussle with the Pentagon. Anthropic had sued the DoD after the department dropped its contract with the company and labeled it a supply chain risk. The company had rejected the Department of War's demand for unrestricted access to its AI models.

However, recently, after Anthropic unveiled its most powerful model, Claude Mythos Preview, a general-purpose frontier model, which it called its "most capable yet for coding and agentic tasks," the Trump Administration seemed to have warmed up a bit. However, the tussle continues.

Michael also told CNBC that Mythos is a separate issue, adding that Mythos is being dealt with government-wide.

Some companies, including OpenAI and SpaceX, had had initial agreements with the Department of War. Earlier this week, it was reported that Google signed a deal allowing the Pentagon to use Google’s AI models on classified work.

Michael said in a statement to the Wall Street Journal that, “We are equipping the warfighter with a suite of AI tools to maintain an unfair advantage and achieve absolute decision superiority."

For many months, Anthropic’s Claude models were some of the only tools available in classified settings through Palantir Technologies (PLTR), which offers AI services to the military through its platform Maven, the report added.

Update: The story was updated with the Department of War CTO's post regarding Oracle joining the AI initiative.
